rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Application And Application For Order Authorizing First Interim Distribution On Allowed Class 2 Policyholder Claims And Third Early Access Distribution To State Insurance Guaranty Funds And Approving Financial Report, Financial Statement And Expenses Of Administration; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Monday, December 09, 2024, line 1, APPELLANT RICARDO LARA INSURANCE COMMISSIONER OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA IN HIS CAPACITY AS LIQUIDATOR OF CASTLEPOINT NATIONAL INSURANCE COMPANY'S Notice Of Application And Application For Order Authorizing First Interim Distribution On Allowed Class 2 Policyholder Claims And Third Early Access Distribution To State Insurance Guaranty Funds And Approving Financial Report, Financial Statement And Expenses Of Administration California Insurance Commissioner Ricardo Lara's Application For Order Authorizing First Interim Distribution On Allowed Class 2 Policyholder Claims And Third Early Access Distribution To State Insurance Guaranty Funds And Approving Financial Report, Financial Statement And Expenses Of Administration, which is unopposed, is GRANTED. Good cause appearing, the court orders: (1) the Commissioner may distribute a total of approximately $3,561,587 from the net assets of the CastlePoint estate on allowed Class 2 policyholder claims as itemized in Exhibit A to the Holloway Declaration; (2) the Commissioner may distribute a total of approximately $16,438,395 of the net assets of the CastlePoint estate to the state insurance guaranty funds or similar entities as itemized in exhibit B to the Holloway Declaration, provided that prior to any such distribution, the state insurance guaranty fund in question has executed and delivered to the Commissioner an agreement, substantially in the form set forth in Exhibit D to the Holloway Declaration, to return the distributed assets as required to pay claims of secured creditors and claims falling within the priorities established in Insurance Code section 1033, subdivisions (a)(1) and (a)(2); (3) the Commissioner's 2023 financial report, Financial Statement, and expenses of administration of the CastlePoint estate are approved; and (4) the Commissioner may take any and all action necessary to carry out the Order requested herein. For the 9:30 a.m. Law & Motion calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (302/JMQ)

rulings.Case Title: WHITNEY GREER PETERSON VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Vacate Judgement Of Dismissal, Pursuant To Ccp 657
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Monday, December 09, 2024, line 2, 2 - PLAINTIFF WHITNEY PETERSON'S Motion To Vacate Judgement Of Dismissal, Pursuant To Ccp 657 Plaintiff Whitney Greer Peterson's Motion To Vacate Judgement Of Dismissal Pursuant To CCP 657 is DENIED. The motion to vacate the judgment is untenable. Plaintiff's motion is based on CCP 657 and 659, but those sections address motions for new trials and notices thereof. To the extent this motion can be understood as a second motion for a new trial, it is denied as an unauthorized successive motion. Further, the motion is substantially identical to Plaintiff's first motion for a new trial and is denied for the same reasons. Further, Plaintiff was required to file her motion within 15 days of the date of mailing notice of entry of judgment by the clerk, which was mailed September 24, 2024. Plaintiff's deadline to file a motion pursuant to CCP 657 was October 9, 2024. Plaintiff filed this motion (without an advance notice) sixteen days after the deadline, on October 25, 2024. Plaintiff's motion is, thus, untimely. CCP 663 and 663a address motions to vacate judgments, but Plaintiff did not file a timely notice under CCP 663a and does not argue the grounds for vacatur under CCP 663 or establish a basis for vacatur under that section. For the 9:30 a.m. Law & Motion calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: ALHASSAN ABUZAID ET AL VS. SALESFORCE INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Deposition Attendance And Production Of Documents Of Pmq #12-15 And Request For Sanctions; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery calendar for Monday, December 9, 2024, Line 2. PLAINTIFFS ALHASSAN ABUZAID, AND REHAB ABOZAYD's Motion To Compel Deposition Attendance And Production Of Documents Of Pmq #12-15 And Request For Sanctions. Plaintiffs Alhassan Abuzaid and Rehab Abozayd's motion to compel deposition attendance and production of documents is granted. In its order of June 24, 2024, the court explained that Defendant Otis Elevator Company's technician was supposed to remedy and/or report dangerous conditions. The PMQ categories #12 through 15 and the production requests relate to Otis' knowledge of the danger and what could have been done to prevent the accident. Plaintiffs' requests meet the lenient discovery standard of Code of Civil Procedure section 2017.010 ["reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence."]; Bank of the Orient v. Superior Court (1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 588, 599 ["section [1151] is a prohibition on the admissibility of evidence at trial. It does not purport to limit the scope of discovery."].) The record on this motion does not show that the subject requests are cumulative. Otis' refusal to produce the PMQ and documents was without substantial justification. Otis shall pay to Plaintiffs sanctions in the amount of $7,200. For the 9:00 a.m. Discovery calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. rulings.Case Title: 2075 SUTTER STREET HOMEOWNERS ASSOCIATION VS. EAST BAY CONSTRUCTION & MANAGEMENT LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For Reconsideration Of The 10 28 24 Order Denying Defendants Motion To Vacate Ccp 1008
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery calendar for Monday, December 9, 2024, Line 3. [Tentative Ruling - Part 1 of 2] DEFENDANT EAST BAY CONSTRUCTION & MANAGEMENT LLC's Motion For Reconsideration Of The 10-28-24 Order Denying Defendants' Motion To Vacate Ccp 1008. Defendant East Bay Construction & Management LLC's motion for reconsideration of motion to vacate order compelling discovery is DENIED. This matter is being heard by a bench officer other than JPT MacDonald because the court has suspended the Discovery JPT program and, thus, JPT MacDonald is not available. First, Defendant fails to demonstrate "new or different facts, circumstances, or law" to warrant reconsideration under CCP 1008(a). Even if Defendant were denied an opportunity for oral argument on the motion to vacate, such is collateral to the merits and not a basis for reconsideration. (Garcia v. Hejmadi (1997) 58 Cal.App.4th 674, 690; Gilberd v. AC Transit (1995) 32 Cal.App.4th 1494, 1500.) Second, a party seeking reconsideration must demonstrate diligence. (Baldwin v. Home Savings of America (1997) 59 Cal.App.4th 1192, 1200.) Here, Defendant did not act diligently. When the court posted its tentative requiring a personal appearance, Defendant failed to email notice of a contest to the tentative, failed request a continuance to enable it to appear in-person and failed to appear to contest that aspect of the ruling. Defendant waived any alleged impropriety of the personal appearance requirement and, in any event, has failed to demonstrate diligence. Third, assuming arguendo that there is good cause to reconsider the order, Defendant fails to demonstrate a basis to alter the October 28, 2024 Order. The discovery motion was correctly decided as was the motion to vacate. Plaintiff properly served counsel of record with the motion to compel and counsel's failure to respond was unreasonable. Defendant complains that plaintiff "stacked" its motion by combining a motion to compel production with a motion to compel responses to interrogatories. While stacking is disfavored, it is not prohibited by the CCP, Rules of Court or Local Rules and, in any event, Defendant did not demonstrate prejudice to Defendant from the practice in this case. Defendant complains that Plaintiff sought sanctions under CCP 2030.210 et seq and 2031.210 et seq. CCP sections 2030.290(c) [failure to respond to interrogatories] and CCP 2031.300(c) [failure to respond to production demand] requires sanctions where a party "unsuccessfully makes or opposes a motion to compel." Defendant argues that sanctions were improper because it never opposed the motion to compel, and Plaintiff failed to cite CRC 3.1348. The argument lacks merit. Plaintiff had to go to the trouble to file a motion to remedy Defendant's discovery abuse. CRC 3.1348(a) clarifies that sanctions are warranted for unopposed discovery motions. The court concludes that Plaintiff's failure specifically to cite CRC 3.1348 in the notice of motion is a matter of form over substance, especially since plaintiff had no way of knowing whether defendant would oppose the motion. Defendant's notice of seeking relief was sufficient. Notably, Defendant's citation to an unpublished opinion in its motion to vacate (CACH, LLC v. Shabbir, 2017 Cal. Super. LEXIS 62682, *1) is not persuasive and violates California Rules of Court, Rule 8.1115(a)). [End of Part 1 of 2]. =(301/JMQ)

rulings.Case Title: LASHEA RODRIGUEZ VS. UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA SAN FRANCISCO, A ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Defendant'S Further Responses To Form Interrogatories, Set One; Request For Sanctions In The Amount Of $2,400; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Declaration Of Erik Harper, Esq.
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery calendar for Monday, December 9, 2024, Line 4. PLAINTIFF LASHEA RODRIGUEZ's Motion To Compel Defendant's Further Responses To Form Interrogatories, Set One; Request For Sanctions In The Amount Of $2,400. Plaintiff Lashea Rodriguez's motion to compel further responses to form interrogatories 12.3 and 12.6 is granted. Plaintiff filed a separate statement that complies with CRC 3.1345 and her meet and confer declaration is sufficient. The subject interrogatories seek the identification of documents, some of which may contain privileged information. Defendant University of California San Francisco correctly notes that it was not obligated to produce a privilege log. (Hernandez v. Superior Court (2003) 112 Cal.App.4th 285, 293.) "But the existence of a document containing privileged information is not privileged. [citations] Interrogatories may be used to discover the existence of documents in the other party's possession. [citations] If an interrogatory asks the responding party to identify a document, an adequate response must include a description of the document." (Id.) Here, Defendant's amended responses generally refer to documents covered by the attorney-client and work product privileges. Defendant does not adequately describe these documents, however. Defendant shall provide supplemental responses that adequately describe the allegedly privileged documents by January 9, 2025. The record does not support a finding that Defendant acted with substantial justification. The court orders sanctions in favor of Plaintiff and against Defense Counsel in the amount of $1,100. For the 9:00 a.m. Discovery calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. rulings.Case Title: PAKAL TECHNOLOGIES, INC. VS. ROBERT BOSCH SEMICONDUCTOR LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: PLAINTIFF PAKAL TECHNOLOGIES, INC. NOTICE OF MOTION AND MOTION TO COMPEL: (1) PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS, AND (2) FURTHER RESPONSES TO SPECIAL INTERROGATORIES.
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery calendar for Monday, December 9, 2024, Line 5. PLAINTIFF PAKAL TECHNOLOGIES, INC.'s MOTION TO COMPEL: (1) PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS, AND (2) FURTHER RESPONSES TO SPECIAL INTERROGATORIES. Plaintiff Pakal Technologies, Inc.'s Motion to Compel (1) Production of Documents, and (2) Further Responses to Special Interrogatories is GRANTED. Plaintiff's RFP Set 2 and SROG No. 2 are reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery admissible evidence on the issues of successor liability and special damages, and good cause appears to compel responses to these discovery requests. Defendant Robert Bosch Semiconductor LLC did not establish that the RFPs are overbroad. Defendant failed to show that any of these requests are unduly burdensome. Defendant's privacy concerns can be addressed by way of protective order. The court does not find that Defendant's response to these discovery requests was substantially justified. The court grants in part Plaintiff's request for sanctions against Defendant and orders Defendant to pay Plaintiff sanctions in the amount of $4,600. The parties are advised that stacking discovery motions is disfavored. Stacked motions may be ordered off calendar in favor of separate motions focused each on a single discovery vehicle (e.g., Frogs, SRogs, RFPs, RFAs, deposition notice). For the 9:00 a.m. Discovery calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number.
